# Get smart about financing smart cities

## Start with these three steps

​US cities are in need of major infrastructure reinvestment and modernization. By embracing new smart city models, we can make our cities more secure, safe, resilient, and globally competitive. Government financial officers can play a key role in enabling this transformation using fiscal policy, Public Private Partnership, and performance-based revenue models. By championing the creation of smart cities, government finance officers can drive urban reinvestment and modernization. This report was developed by Deloitte US.

### The promise of smart cities

​There is growing momentum around the smart city as a viable model for rejuvenating urban economies and societies. Smart cities leverage an infrastructure powered by modern technologies such as the Internet of Things, embedded sensors, and cloud-based services. Because of this, they are more resilient and environmentally friendly. Perhaps most importantly, smart cities can help government leaders reduce costs and increase revenues while better serving citizens.

[Back to top](#top)

### Creative approaches are required

​To make the vision of smart cities a reality, government finance officers will have to find creative alternatives to traditional infrastructure financing models. Under their stewardship, municipalities can seek out cross-government collaboration and innovative partnerships that provide new sources of investment. Public–private partnerships (PPPs), revenue sharing agreements, and pay-for-performance arrangements are examples of resourceful new approaches to funding and financing smart cities. Investment vehicles like these go beyond traditional debt instruments to ensure less risk and more reward for all stakeholders.

[Back to top](#top)

### Advancing smart cities: Three key steps

​Government financial officers have an important role to play in the development smart cities. Here are three steps state and city leaders should consider as they look to finance smart city initiatives:

[Back to top](#top)

### Get on a smarter path: New partnership models for smart cities

​Regardless of the technology application, a cross-cutting theme in smart city infrastructure investment is the re-allocation of risk and reward between the public and private sectors. Federal, state, and local leaders will increasingly encounter new partnership models for front-end investment and revenue sharing, including pay-for-performance related to service improvements or access to services.

Even resource-constrained federal, state, and local entities can participate in the emerging smart cities market with the oversight of their financial, accounting, and infrastructure leaders. As in the case of the Smart Cities Challenge sponsored by the U.S. Department of Transportation (DoT), winning city Columbus, Ohio, received a $40 million DoT grant, along with $10 million in support from Vulcan Philanthropies. The private sector also stepped forward with $100 million in investment. Collectively, this investment will be used to transform how transportation is used in Columbus. One area of focus is ride-sharing that can better connect lower-income communities to their city, which can result in economic benefits, citizen inclusion and potentially better health outcomes.  
  
By thinking outside the box and partnering in new ways, even resource-constrained federal, state, and local governments can participate in smart city programs. And as more municipalities pursue smart city strategies, valuable lessons and best practices will enable the success of future adopters.

Download our new report Funding and financing smart cities—from the Deloitte Center for Government Insights, published in the Association of Government Accountants’ Journal of Government Financial Management—to learn more about smart cities in action and the innovative financing strategies behind them.

## Cities

# [5 Examples of Smart City Partnerships That the Public and Private Sectors Can Engage In](/en/cities/5-examples-of-smart-city-partnerships-that-the-public-and-private-sectors-can-engage-in "5 Examples of Smart City Partnerships That the Public and Private Sectors Can Engage In")

![logo](https://www.thesmartcityjournal.com//templates/ja_focus/images/logo.png)
![5 Examples of Smart City Partnerships That the Public and Private Sectors Can Engage In](/images/Imagenes-Articulos/2024_05_Mayo/Smart-City-Partnerships.jpg)

By leveraging the expertise and resources of both sectors, PPPs enable cities to tackle complex urban challenges, harness technology to improve service delivery and create more resilient and livable urban environments

Public-private partnerships (PPPs) play a pivotal role in driving the development and implementation of innovative projects that contribute to the realisation of smart city goals. By leveraging the expertise and resources of both sectors, PPPs enable cities to tackle complex urban challenges, harness technology to improve service delivery, and create more resilient and livable urban environments. Below are examples of PPP-supported projects that can lead to the development of smarter, safer, and more efficient cities for the future.

## Smart Public Safety and Security

It’s crucial for any modern city to prevent crime and improve emergency response capabilities. This can be achieved with the wide adoption of [smart city technology](https://www.tech.gov.sg/capability-centre-sctd) and systems that are designed to improve public safety and security. Supported by both the public and private sectors, these projects are crucial for building resilient and secure smart cities that prioritise the safety and well-being of residents and visitors.

The Safe City Project in Singapore serves as a compelling example of how public-private partnerships can drive the development and implementation of innovative safety and security solutions. It leverages advanced technologies such as data analytics and surveillance cameras and sensors to carry out real-time monitoring and analysis of urban environments. This makes it easy to detect and respond to security threats effectively.

By investing in smart surveillance and monitoring systems, urban authorities can deter criminal activity, improve their situational awareness, and enhance their emergency response capabilities. Moreover, by leveraging data analytics and predictive modelling, authorities can identify emerging threats and allocate resources more effectively to mitigate risks to public safety.

## Smart Transportation Systems

Smart transportation systems play a vital role in the development of smart cities. These systems make it possible to improve mobility, reduce traffic congestion, and enhance the overall transportation experience for urban residents. Infrastructure projects such as the Gautrain Rapid Rail Link in South Africa exemplify how PPPs can facilitate the deployment of innovative transportation solutions.

A joint project between the South African government and the private sector, the 80-kilometre commuter rail system required the construction of new railway lines, stations, depots, and associated infrastructure, including state-of-the-art signalling and communications systems. It also incorporates various smart transportation features, including high-speed trains, integrated ticketing and fare collection systems, real-time passenger information displays, and park-and-ride facilities.

By bringing together the public and private sectors, smart transportation projects can address pressing urban challenges while delivering tangible benefits to society. Since its inauguration in 2010, the Gautrain Rapid Rail Link has significantly improved mobility and connectivity in Gauteng Province, reducing travel times, relieving congestion on major roadways, and providing a safe and comfortable transportation option for millions of passengers annually. The project has also spurred economic development and investment along its route, contributing to the region's growth and prosperity.

## Smart Energy Grids

An essential component of smart cities, smart energy grids make it possible to integrate renewable energy sources, improve energy efficiency, and enhance grid reliability and resilience. The Smart Grid Investment Grant (SGIG) program in the United States illustrates how public-private partnerships can drive the development and deployment of smart grid technologies nationwide. Through partnerships between utility companies, technology providers, and government agencies, the SGIG program aims to modernise the electric grid and promote sustainable energy practices.

Collaborating with the private sector allows governments to leverage the expertise and resources of industry leaders to accelerate the deployment of smart grid technologies and achieve energy sustainability goals. By investing in smart energy grids, cities can reduce carbon emissions, enhance energy security, and create new opportunities for economic growth and innovation. Moreover, smart grids enable greater integration of renewable energy sources such as solar and wind power, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and mitigating the impacts of climate change.

## Smart Water Management

Smart water management is critical for ensuring the efficient and sustainable use of water resources in urban environments. Projects such as the Thames Tideway Tunnel in London, United Kingdom, demonstrate how PPPs can drive the development of smart water infrastructure to address pressing challenges such as sewage overflow and water pollution. Partnering with private companies allows governments to leverage innovative technologies and financing mechanisms and deliver cost-effective solutions that improve water quality and protect the environment.

Through the deployment of sensor networks, data analytics, and predictive maintenance technologies, smart water management projects enable cities to monitor water networks in real time. This makes it possible to identify potential issues and optimise resource allocation. Cities that invest in smart water infrastructure are positioned to enhance resilience to climate change, reduce water waste, and ensure access to clean and safe drinking water for all residents.

## Smart Waste Management

**City authorities** need effective waste management to maintain healthy and sustainable urban environments. Smart waste management projects, such as the Cleanaway Waste Management Facility in Sydney, Australia, leverage technology to simplify collection, processing, and disposal processes, thereby reducing environmental impact and improving resource efficiency.

By collaborating with private waste management companies, governments can deploy advanced technologies such as sensor-based monitoring systems and route optimisation software to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of waste collection services. These technologies enable cities to minimise waste generation, and they can also contribute to increasing recycling rates and reducing landfill emissions. Moreover, by engaging the private sector, governments can access additional funding and expertise to implement innovative solutions that address complex urban challenges and deliver long-term benefits to society.

As the global population continues to urbanise and cities face increasingly complex challenges, the concept of smart cities has emerged as a beacon of hope for creating more sustainable, inclusive, and resilient urban environments. However, building a smart city is no small feat; it requires the collective effort and commitment of both the public and private sectors. By embracing collaboration and harnessing the power of public-private partnerships, cities can pave the way towards a brighter, smarter future where urban communities have a better chance to thrive and provide better quality of life for all.

--- SOURCE 5: Public-private partnerships in smart cities: A critical survey ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1877916622000522

SUMMARY:
by X Quan · 2023 · Cited by 45 — In theory and practice, collaboration is pivotal for effective urban development, most often seen through the establishment of public-private partnership (PPP).



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://hylan.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Public-Private-Partnerships-in-Smart-City-Development-2.png
DESCRIPTION: A city skyline with a focus on smart city infrastructure, featuring a digital network overlay and the partnership between Hylan and other stakeholders for urban development.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://stealth-iss.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Smart-City-Intelligent-Platforms.png
DESCRIPTION: A detailed schematic illustrates various intelligent platforms for a smart city, highlighting systems for healthcare, security, transportation, education, and governance that work collaboratively to enhance urban living.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Monotch-partnership-1-1024x1024.jpg
DESCRIPTION: Two individuals in business attire shake hands with a background of parked cars and a digital grid overlay, symbolizing a partnership in smart city mobility and digital infrastructure.

IMAGE 4:
URL: http://adtellintegration.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/05/smart-cities-infrastructure-iot-wide.png
DESCRIPTION: A detailed illustration of a smart city concept featuring various elements such as traffic management, smart buildings, public safety, and electric vehicle charging, all interconnected through modern technology.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://highways.today/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Building-Smart-Cities.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A modern cityscape with interconnected digital icons representing smart city infrastructure, connectivity, and partnerships, highlighted by a sunset glow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Partnering with tech firms harnesses innovative solutions and expertise, enhancing smart city infrastructure through advanced technologies like IoT and data analytics.
Involving citizens in planning fosters ownership and ensures smart city initiatives meet community needs, leading to greater acceptance and successful implementation.
Collaboration with universities drives research and development in smart technologies, providing data-driven insights and innovative solutions for urban challenges.
Non-profit organizations can bridge gaps in community engagement and funding, ensuring smart city projects are inclusive and address social needs effectively.

enriched_points.point_title: Creating a network of smart cities
enriched_points.expanded_content: Establishing connections between cities promotes knowledge sharing and collaboration, fostering innovation and scalability of successful smart city solutions globally.
